step_by_step_thinking:
\boxed{A}
The evidence consistently shows that vitamin K acts as a crucial cofactor in the γ-carboxylation of clotting factors, enabling them to become functionally active. Prothrombin is one of these vitamin K-dependent clotting factors, so vitamin K is necessary for its production and subsequent participation in the coagulation cascade. Therefore, option A correctly reflects the primary physiological role of vitamin K in hemostasis. While some entries mention links between vitamin K and antifibrinolytics, those are either indirect or refer to pharmacological agents structurally related to vitamin K—not intrinsic functions of vitamin K itself. Options B, C, and D lack clear support from the provided data.
